images.caption: Figure 1:From a low-dimensional seed we produce dense, regular grids via analytic parametrizations or systematic transforms; central finite differences recover geometric estimators such as reach and curvature enabling validation of manifold-fitting bounds.

Figure 2:Scalar-curvature RMSE (lower is better) versus number of grid samples, comparing our finite-difference estimator (blue) with(Sritharanet al.,2021)(orange) on hyperspheres of multiple dimensionsS2,S3,S4S^{2},S^{3},S^{4}, the hyperboloidH22H^{2}_{2}, and the torusT2T^{2}.

Figure 3:Empirical Hausdorff error against theoretical bounds on dSprites for d = 1 to 4, using MMLS (linear local fit). Empirical mean ±σ\sigmaover 3 repetitions in blue, x-axis is training-set fraction (%). Genovese lower bound in dotted green, Genovese upper in solid green, Fefferman upper in orange. Constants fitted to envelope the empirical curve. At d = 2 the Genovese upper and Fefferman bounds overlap.

Figure 5:Hausdorff error and bounds for MMLS on, from left to right, SphereS2S^{2}, TorusT2T^{2}, extended COIL-20, and balanced dSprites. Empirical mean ±σ\sigmaover 3 repetitions, x-axis is training-set fraction (%). Please note that forS2S^{2}andT2T^{2}, the Genovese upper and Fefferman bounds are the same ford=2d=2since we use the same fitting process for their respective multiplicative constants.
Figure 4:Hausdorff error and bounds on 4D dSprites for MMLS (left) andβ\beta-VAE (right; model B, latent dim 10,β\beta= 4). Empirical mean ±σ\sigmaover 3 repetitions. Bounds as in Figure 3.

Figure 6:Evolution of manifold geometry across the layers of aβ\beta-VAE.For each layer (input→\rightarrowencoder→\rightarrowlatentμ→\mu\rightarrowdecoder→\rightarrowoutput; encoder and decoder halves shaded blue and orange) we report six
geometric measures: volume, scalar curvature, its negative and positive parts, reach,
and average pairwise distance (all normalized except volume).Top:dSprites, one curve per object class (square, ellipse, heart).Bottom:COIL-20, summarized as the mean over the 20 object classes
(solid line) with a±1​σ\pm 1\sigmaband (shaded: one standard deviation across classes).
The full per-class COIL-20 version is given in Fig.31.

header: Appendix FRasterization Effects and Metric Smoothing on a Two-Dimensional Ellipse Manifold

images.caption: Figure 17:Example images from the two-dimensional ellipse grid generated by varying horizontal translationxxand rotationθ\theta.
Figure 18:Estimated metric componentg00g_{00}for the ellipse grid. The top row uses the direct finite-difference metric; the bottom row smooths the metric tensor before computing derived quantities.
Figure 19:Estimated metric componentg11g_{11}for the ellipse grid, with the same layout as Fig.18.
Figure 20:Estimated volume element on the ellipse grid. Rasterization effects are milder than for curvature but are still visible at high grid densities.
Figure 21:Estimated scalar curvature on the ellipse grid. Curvature is the most sensitive of the displayed quantities because it depends on further finite differences of the metric.

Figure 22:UMAP projection of the ellipse images colored by the estimated measures. The top row uses the direct finite-difference estimates and the bottom row uses metric smoothing. The UMAP coordinates are identical across rows.
